title: Best-of-N Sampling
concept: Generating N candidates and selecting the best one via a scoring function or reward model.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: why does majority voting tend to improve accuracy across multiple model samples?

options.text: Because errors tend to be random and different each time, while the correct reasoning path shows up more consistently, so it wins the vote
Because the model gets smarter each time it generates a new sample
Because longer answers are always more accurate than short ones

exercises.explain: Right! Mistakes tend to be scattered and inconsistent across samples, but the correct path tends to reappear because there's often only one (or few) correct ways to solve a problem. That consistency lets the correct answer win the majority vote.

cards.title: What If You Could Just... Pick the Winner?
cards.body: Majority voting works great when the right answer is common. But what if the best answer is rare, or there's no clear 'majority' at all? What if you had a judge who could just tell you which answer is actually good?

cards.title: What Is Best-of-N Sampling?
cards.body: Best-of-N means asking the model to generate N different candidate answers, then using a scoring function or reward model to judge each one and pick the single best. Instead of counting votes, you're grading quality.

cards.title: A Chef Cooking Off
cards.body: Imagine 8 chefs each cook their own version of a dish, but a food critic tastes all 8 and picks the winner. You don't need every chef to be great — you just need one great dish and a critic who can tell it's the best.

cards.title: Grading Essays, Not Counting Them
cards.body: Ask an AI to write 6 different opening paragraphs for an essay. A reward model (trained to judge writing quality) scores each one from 0 to 10. You keep the paragraph with the highest score — even if the other 5 were totally different from it.

cards.title: Myth: Best-of-N Is Just Majority Voting Renamed
cards.body: Not true! Majority voting picks the most FREQUENT answer among samples. Best-of-N picks the highest-SCORING answer using a separate judge. A great answer can win Best-of-N even if it only appeared once.

cards.title: Counting Votes vs. Scoring Quality
cards.body: These two ensembling strategies solve the same problem — picking one good answer from many — but in very different ways.

left.text: Generate N answers, pick whichever answer appears MOST OFTEN. No judge needed, just counting.

right.text: Generate N answers, use a SCORER or reward model to pick the highest-quality one, even if it's rare.

exercises.prompt: What does Best-of-N sampling require that plain majority voting does not?

options.text: A way to score or judge the quality of each candidate answer
All candidates must be exactly the same
The model must be run only once
A human must write every candidate by hand

exercises.explain: Majority voting just needs answers to compare and count. Best-of-N needs an extra ingredient: a scoring function or reward model that can actually judge which candidate is best, even if it's the only one of its kind.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In Best-of-N sampling, the winning candidate must also be the most common answer among the N generated candidates.
exercises.explain: False. Best-of-N picks the highest-scoring candidate according to the scoring function or reward model — that candidate doesn't need to repeat at all. It could be a unique answer that simply scores best.

exercises.sentence: In Best-of-N sampling, a scoring function or reward model evaluates each candidate to find the best one.
exercises.explain: The reward model (or scoring function) is the judge in Best-of-N — it evaluates quality directly, which is different from majority voting's vote-counting approach.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scoring approaches is most likely AI-generated (a reward model's output) rather than a simple rule a human wrote?

options.text: A trained model outputs a score of 8.7 for how 'helpful and well-reasoned' a response sounds, based on patterns learned from human preference data
A script counts how many test cases a piece of code passes out of 20
A script checks if the final numeric answer matches the known correct answer

exercises.explain: Options B and C are simple, deterministic rules a human could easily code (count passing tests, check exact match). Option A describes a learned reward model — it produces a nuanced quality judgment based on patterns from training data, which is a hallmark of AI-generated scoring rather than a hand-written rule.

exercises.prompt: An AI writes code to solve a tricky bug. You generate 10 candidate fixes with Best-of-N and use a scoring function that runs each fix against unit tests. Which statement is TRUE about this setup?

options.text: The final answer is whichever candidate the model wrote first
The scorer (unit tests) picks the candidate that actually passes the tests, not just the most common one
Best-of-N requires all 10 candidates to be identical to work
Best-of-N only works for math problems, never for code

exercises.explain: This is the key difference from majority voting: instead of counting which answer appears most often, Best-of-N uses an actual scoring function (here, unit tests) to judge quality directly. A correct fix could be the only one of its kind among 10 candidates and still win, because it's the one that passes the tests — frequency doesn't matter, quality does. Best-of-N also generalizes far beyond math: code, essays, images, anything you can score works.
